questions.question:

“I closed my eyes. I saw Dad standing on a stage, his head bowed, his name written in large black letters, and then crossed out in red ink, on a sign hanging from his neck. I saw myself standing in the middle of the stage, facing thousands of people, condemning Dad for his crimes, raising my fist to lead the chant, ‘Down with Jiang Xi-reng.’ I saw Dad looking at me hopelessly, tears on his face.”

What does this suggest about Ji-li's relationship with her father?

questions.question: What is a central idea from Chapter 2 of Freedom Walkers?

responses.content: D. Whites were careful not to target blacks who they thought could provide a challenge to segregation down the line.

questions.question: Which sentence from the text most strongly supports the correct answer to the previous question?

responses.content: D. “At school, Claudette had been studying the U.S. Constitution and the Bill of Rights, and she had taken those lessons to heart.”

activities.text_title: Freedom’s Daughters: The Unsung Heroines of the Civil Rights Movement from 1830 to 1970

questions.question: How was Barbara Johns similar to her uncle Vernon?
